responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing clinical rigor with naturalistic observation, data quality with participant comfort, and operational scalability with safety constraints. Critical levers must address these conflicts while adhering to strict ethical and methodological boundaries. The selected levers target foundational trade-offs in facility design, staffing, data governance, recruitment, and technological integration, ensuring the unit's viability as a hybrid clinical-research environment without overreaching into diagnostic or commercial domains.

levers.name: Residential Environment Optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standard safety protocols with padded edges and restricted windows → Systemic: 15% higher renovation costs but 30% reduced false alarm rates due to controlled stimuli → Strategic: Prioritizes physiological fidelity over clinical sterility, but risks participant alienation if perceived as overly institutionalized. Controls safety vs. comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs.
options: Implement standard safety protocols with padded edges and restricted windows
Adopt modular renovation strategies to allow phased expansion
Integrate AI-driven environmental monitoring for real-time risk assessment
levers.review_lever: Controls safety vs. comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs.

levers.name: Staffing Ratio Configuration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintain 3:8 technician-to-suite ratios → Systemic: 20% higher labor costs but 40% faster event response times → Strategic: Ensures safety but limits scalability; requires trade-off between operational depth and financial sustainability. Controls efficiency vs. cost. Weakness: The options fail to account for staff burnout thresholds.
options: Maintain 3:8 technician-to-suite ratios for direct oversight
Deploy shift-optimized 2:8 ratios with AI-assisted alert triage
Introduce peer-led monitoring teams with shared responsibility models
levers.review_lever: Controls efficiency vs. cost. Weakness: The options fail to account for staff burnout thresholds.

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Immediate: Real-time technician annotation with dual-rater verification → Systemic: 25% slower throughput but 90% inter-rater reliability → Strategic: Strengthens data validity but creates bottleneck for large-scale expansion. Controls accuracy vs. throughput. Weakness: The options fail to address annotation bias from technician training variability.
options: Enforce real-time technician annotation with dual-rater verification
Develop tiered annotation protocols prioritizing high-frequency events
Implement crowdsourced annotation with AI-assisted validation filters
levers.review_lever: Controls accuracy vs. throughput. Weakness: The options fail to address annotation bias from technician training variability.

levers.name: Recruitment Criteria Refinement
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict exclusion of epilepsy mimics and psychiatric instability → Systemic: 10% lower enrollment but 50% higher event capture rates → Strategic: Enhances data purity but risks underrepresentation of comorbid populations. Controls specificity vs. generalizability. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional demographic disparities.
options: Maintain strict exclusion criteria for neurological and psychiatric comorbidities
Introduce phased inclusion for stable comorbid cases with clinical oversight
Establish community-based screening partnerships with local sleep clinics
levers.review_lever: Controls specificity vs. generalizability. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional demographic disparities.

levers.name: Participant Compensation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: €80 per night plus meals for all participants → Systemic: 20% higher retention but 15% increased budget strain → Strategic: Balances incentive with fiscal responsibility, but may attract non-representative cohorts. Controls motivation vs. representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to quantify compensation's impact on self-selection bias.
options: Standardize €80 per night plus meals for all participants
Introduce tiered compensation based on event yield and stay duration
Offer non-monetary incentives like personalized sleep reports and follow-up care
levers.review_lever: Controls motivation vs. representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to quantify compensation's impact on self-selection bias.

levers.name: Sensor Data Integration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deploy dry-electrode EEG headbands with contact-free mattress sensors → Systemic: 35% lower participant compliance but 50% richer physiological data → Strategic: Improves technical resolution but may compromise natural behavior. Controls resolution vs. compliance. Weakness: The options fail to address sensor interoperability challenges.
options: Prioritize low-burden dry-electrode EEG headbands with mattress sensors
Introduce hybrid sensor arrays with optional participant calibration
Adopt contactless radar-based motion tracking as a non-intrusive alternative
levers.review_lever: Controls resolution vs. compliance. Weakness: The options fail to address sensor interoperability challenges.

levers.name: Ethical Oversight Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintain dual human scorer annotation with AASM criteria → Systemic: 25% slower publication timelines but 95% ethical compliance rate → Strategic: Reinforces methodological integrity but delays knowledge dissemination. Controls rigor vs. speed. Weakness: The options fail to incorporate participant feedback mechanisms.
options: Enforce dual human scorer annotation with AASM criteria
Introduce iterative ethics reviews with participant advisory panels
Develop anonymized participant dashboards for real-time data transparency
levers.review_lever: Controls rigor vs. speed. Weakness: The options fail to incorporate participant feedback mechanisms.

responses.summary: The levers effectively address operational and methodological tensions but lack consideration for long-term participant engagement beyond compensation. Add 'Develop anonymized participant dashboards for real-time data transparency' to Ethical Oversight Framework to balance accountability with behavioral naturalism.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing clinical rigor with participant comfort, data fidelity with operational scalability, and ethical compliance with research agility. Critical levers must address physical environment constraints, staffing dynamics, and data capture limitations while navigating regulatory and resource boundaries. The selected levers target foundational operational decisions that directly influence feasibility, validity, and long-term adaptability of the residential model.

levers.name: Environmental Acoustics Management
levers.consequences: Immediate: Soundproofing upgrades reduce ambient noise interference → Systemic: 25% improvement in sleep stage detection accuracy per pilot metrics → Strategic: Enhances physiological data integrity but increases renovation costs by €120K per suite
options: Implement standard acoustic insulation with fiberglass panels and double-glazed windows
Deploy adaptive noise cancellation systems with real-time frequency filtering
Integrate machine learning-driven acoustic adjustments using IoT sensor arrays
levers.review_lever: Controls noise suppression vs. cost efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant psychological comfort thresholds.

levers.name: Participant Engagement Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Structured daily debriefs increase self-report accuracy → Systemic: 18% higher event capture rates in pilot cohorts → Strategic: Creates dependency on human interaction but reduces automated annotation reliability
options: Conduct morning interviews with participants to validate nocturnal experiences
Introduce biweekly progress journals with narrative prompts
Establish peer mentorship networks between participants and control groups
levers.review_lever: Controls structured interaction vs. autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for participant fatigue in extended stays.

levers.name: Sensor Fusion Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Multi-modal sensor calibration reduces data gaps → Systemic: 30% faster event triage during pilot → Strategic: Creates technical debt in integration workflows but enables cross-validation of anomalies
options: Standardize sensor triggers to a single time-stamping protocol
Develop hybrid alert thresholds combining motion and audio data
Implement real-time distributed data logging with edge computing nodes
levers.review_lever: Controls technical standardization vs. flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to address interoperability with future neuroimaging systems.

levers.name: Control Group Benchmarking
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standardized control protocols improve comparison validity → Systemic: 15% reduction in false-positive event rates → Strategic: Increases baseline data complexity but limits control group size to 20% of total enrollment
options: Apply identical monitoring parameters to control and parasomnia groups
Introduce variable sleep schedules for control participants to mimic real-world diversity
Use longitudinal control cohorts with periodic re-enrollment cycles
levers.review_lever: Controls uniformity vs. variability. Weakness: The options fail to account for control group attrition over extended periods.

levers.name: Staff Shift Rotation Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: 12-hour shifts reduce technician fatigue → Systemic: 40% lower false alarm rates in pilot data → Strategic: Creates scheduling complexity but improves real-time annotation quality
options: Implement fixed 12-hour night shifts with mandatory rest periods
Adopt staggered 8-hour shifts with overlapping handoff windows
Introduce predictive workload modeling using historical event patterns
levers.review_lever: Controls predictability vs. adaptability. Weakness: The options fail to consider shift-related communication breakdowns during handoffs.

levers.name: Ethical Compliance Workflow
levers.consequences: Immediate: Automated consent tracking reduces administrative burden → Systemic: 50% faster ethics approval cycles → Strategic: Creates audit trail dependencies but limits rapid protocol adjustments
options: Maintain paper-based consent documentation with physical signatures
Deploy blockchain-secured digital consent logs with timestamped revisions
Establish continuous ethics monitoring through real-time data access controls
levers.review_lever: Controls documentation transparency vs. regulatory flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-border data governance for German participants.

levers.name: Regulatory Adaptation Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Proactive stakeholder consultations accelerate approvals → Systemic: 35% reduction in compliance-related delays → Strategic: Creates institutional dependency but enables rapid protocol iteration
options: Follow standard IRB review timelines with sequential approvals
Formulate expedited review panels with hospital legal advisors
Develop dynamic regulatory mapping using historical compliance data trends
levers.review_lever: Controls procedural speed vs. regulatory adherence. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving EU sleep disorder classification standards.

responses.summary: These levers address critical operational and ethical dimensions but lack focus on participant mental health support. Add 'Participant Psychological Support Integration' to the Staff Shift Rotation Model lever to mitigate long-term sleep disruption effects and improve retention rates.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing clinical rigor with participant comfort, data fidelity with operational efficiency, and regulatory compliance with rapid iteration. The selected levers address critical gaps in community integration, data governance, and adaptive infrastructure, ensuring the facility's sustainability while mitigating risks of over-reliance on rigid protocols. By prioritizing these levers, the project navigates the conflict between methodological precision and real-world feasibility, fostering both scientific validity and ethical stewardship.

options: Implement daily manual recalibration with technician logs

levers.review_lever: Controls technical reliability vs. operational complexity. Weakness: The options fail to account for environmental variability in sleep suites.

options: Use paper-based nightly feedback forms with structured prompts
Introduce bi-weekly focus groups for qualitative insights

levers.review_lever: Controls participant comfort vs. data objectivity. Weakness: The options fail to address potential survey fatigue in long-term stays.

levers.name: Regulatory Agility Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Streamlines ethics committee interactions through standardized templates → Systemic: Cuts approval cycles by 20% for protocol amendments → Strategic: Accelerates innovation but risks regulatory noncompliance during rapid iterations. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data transfer complexities.
options: Maintain static ethics documentation with annual reviews

Adopt a modular ethics approval system for parallel protocol testing
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory speed vs. compliance rigor. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data transfer complexities.

levers.name: Cross-Training Operational Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Reduces staffing bottlenecks through skill-sharing → Systemic: Increases technician flexibility by 30% during shift changes → Strategic: Weakens domain specialization but enhances crisis response capacity. options: Require technicians to master all sensor systems and safety protocols
Rotate staff between monitoring and annotation roles monthly
Develop a tiered certification system for role-specific emergency response
levers.review_lever: Controls operational flexibility vs. expertise depth. Weakness: The options fail to quantify cognitive load on multi-skilled staff.

levers.name: Environmental Resilience Testing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Simulates extreme conditions via controlled stressors → Systemic: Identifies 25% more safety vulnerabilities during pilot phase → Strategic: Builds robust infrastructure but diverts resources from primary research. Integrate real-time environmental monitoring for microclimate adjustments
Partner with local disaster response teams for scenario-based drills
levers.review_lever: Controls safety preparedness vs. research focus. Weakness: The options fail to address long-term maintenance costs of stress testing.

levers.name: Data Stewardship Consortium
levers.consequences: Immediate: Assigns data access tiers to stakeholders → Systemic: Reduces unauthorized access incidents by 40% through role-based controls → Strategic: Encourages collaborative analysis but creates friction in data sharing. Weakness: The options fail to account for evolving legal standards in neurodata governance.

Establish a federated data model with localized processing nodes
levers.review_lever: Controls data accessibility vs. collaboration efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to account for evolving legal standards in neurodata governance.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
